progressive__claude-opus-4-6.label: Progressive (claude-opus-4-6)
progressive__claude-opus-4-6.overall_feedback: This paper investigates an important and timely question—how radiologists interact with AI diagnostic tools in real clinical practice—and brings impressive data assets to bear, including over 100,000 scans, nearly 400 radiologists, and a staggered rollout design. The descriptive findings on asymmetric agreement rates, evolving disagreement over time, and heterogeneity across radiologists are richly detailed and valuable for understanding human-AI collaboration beyond laboratory settings. However, the paper faces significant challenges in drawing causal or normative conclusions from its observational design. Most critically, the authors acknowledge but do not resolve a fundamental identification problem: differences in patient outcomes across agreement/disagreement categories conflate diagnostic accuracy, patient severity, and treatment responses. Without a ground truth for PE presence (e.g., from independent adjudication or autopsy data), it is difficult to determine whether radiologist overrides of AI improve or worsen diagnostic quality, and the mortality comparisons across concordance categories are susceptible to selection bias—sicker patients may systematically sort into certain disagreement cells. The staggered rollout, while promising for identification, does not appear to be leveraged with a formal difference-in-differences or event-study framework that would credibly separate the causal effect of AI introduction from secular trends in scan volume, patient mix, or radiologist experience. Additionally, the finding that female radiologists are less likely to override AI, while intriguing, risks being confounded by specialization, experience, or case mix differences unless more carefully controlled. The paper would benefit from more disciplined causal reasoning—perhaps exploiting the staggered adoption as an instrument or natural experiment—and from being more explicit about what its descriptive associations can and cannot tell us about optimal human-AI collaboration in clinical medicine.

comments.title: Ambiguous and potentially misleading claim that 'none' of the follow-up scans maintain the AI-positive/radiologist-negative disagreement pattern
comments.quote: When radiologists initially reject AI positive flags, none of the follow-up scans maintain that disagreement pattern.
comments.explanation: This claim appears multiple times (abstract, introduction, Section 7, conclusion) and is extremely strong (literally 0%). The paper never provides the denominator for this specific cell. Given that only 3.8% of patients return for follow-up and AI Only (+) is only 1.7% of initial scans, the cell count could be as few as ~26 cases. A single additional observation would change the finding. Additionally, the phrasing is ambiguous: the text later states 30.8% of AI Only (+) cases transition to Agree (+), meaning the radiologist does eventually agree with a positive PE finding on follow-up — contradicting a natural reading of 'no follow-up scan shows the radiologist changing to agree with the AI.' The distinction between persistence of the exact disagreement pattern vs. eventual radiologist agreement with any positive AI call is not clearly drawn. The abstract should qualify this or note the sample size.

comments.title: Conflation of 'time from order to diagnosis' with 'reading time' and inconsistent characterization as 'stable'
comments.quote: The average time from order to diagnosis holds steady at 3.6 hours per scan even as total scan volume increased 16%.
comments.explanation: Multiple issues are intertwined: (1) 'Time from order to diagnosis' includes queuing, scheduling, and waiting — it is not 'reading time,' yet the paper uses the terms interchangeably ('The stable per-scan reading time'). Table 6 shows actual median time-to-finalization of 9.1 minutes. (2) The order-to-diagnosis time actually increases significantly from 3.57 to 3.72 hours (se=0.07, t>2), yet the abstract calls it 'stable at 3.6 hours.' (3) PE-positive reading time increases from 3.2 to 3.6 hours, converging to the overall average. This implies negative-case times must have decreased, but this compositional result is never acknowledged. (4) The paper cites a meta-analysis claiming AI reduces reading time by 27%, yet their own data shows no such reduction — this tension is not discussed. The conflation of these distinct metrics and the characterization of a statistically significant increase as 'stable' could mislead readers.

comments.title: Mortality and outcome comparisons presented without uncertainty measures or adequate causal caveats
comments.quote: When only the radiologist diagnoses PE, mortality increases 5.5 percentage points. When only the AI detects PE but the radiologist does not, mortality increases 4.0 percentage points.
comments.explanation: The abstract presents mortality differences as precise point estimates without confidence intervals. The outcome regression (Eq. 3) includes only site, time-of-day×day-of-week, and year fixed effects — no patient-level covariates, despite collecting 60 patient control variables. Agreement categories are endogenous (depending on patient severity and radiologist behavior), so coefficients conflate diagnostic agreement effects with patient severity selection. Furthermore, the paper inconsistently uses causal language: 'Radiologist overrides to diagnose PE lead to anticoagulation treatment' implies causality, while elsewhere the authors appropriately use 'associate with.' The authors acknowledge planned future causal analysis, but the current presentation could lead readers to over-interpret these observational associations.

comments.title: 'Five years of AI use' claim is inconsistent with the August 2019–July 2022 study period
comments.quote: large heterogeneity persists across radiologists even after five years of AI use
comments.explanation: The study period runs from August 2019 to July 2022 — approximately three years. With staggered rollout, most sites had AI for substantially less than three years. The claim of 'five years of AI use' appears in both the related literature section and the data description section. This is a notable overstatement of the observation window that could mislead readers about the depth of longitudinal evidence.

comments.title: Abstract's 'override AI' phrasing is ambiguous in direction
comments.quote: female radiologists are 6 percentage points less likely to override AI than male radiologists
comments.explanation: The introduction clarifies this refers specifically to overriding AI-detected PEs (AI-positive flags rejected by the radiologist), but the abstract's version ('override AI') is ambiguous — it could mean overriding AI in either direction. Given the asymmetric agreement patterns, the direction matters substantially.

comments.title: Recurrent PE rates (48.5%, 55.3%) are implausibly high and likely reflect surveillance bias rather than true recurrence
comments.quote: Recurrent PE shows larger differences: when only the radiologist diagnoses PE, the rate is 48.5%, approaching the 55.3% rate when both agree on PE presence.
comments.explanation: These rates are far higher than clinical literature norms (typically 2-5% within 30 days for treated PE). The R² for the recurrent PE regression is 0.387, dramatically higher than all other outcomes (0.005-0.073), suggesting a near-mechanical relationship with initial diagnosis. Patients diagnosed with PE receive follow-up imaging that detects residual/ongoing PE, which is coded as 'recurrent.' The authors partially acknowledge this but still present it as a 'Primary Clinical Outcome' alongside mortality. The variable is fundamentally a measure of clinical follow-up intensity rather than a genuine health outcome, and the definition of 'recurrent PE' is never clarified. This could seriously mislead readers interpreting it at face value.

comments.title: NLP-based PE diagnosis extraction is critically underspecified
comments.quote: We extracted radiology reports containing radiologist names from OMOP note tables using natural language processing to identify PE diagnoses.
comments.explanation: The radiologist's PE diagnosis is one of the two core variables in the entire study, yet the NLP method is described in a single clause. There is no mention of the algorithm type, validation against manual chart review, sensitivity/specificity, handling of equivocal language ('cannot exclude PE'), or inter-annotator agreement. Misclassification would directly bias all reported agreement rates and outcome comparisons. A reader cannot assess whether the 97% concordance on AI-negatives could partly reflect NLP errors.

comments.title: LLM-based radiologist name matching procedure is underspecified
comments.quote: We matched radiologist names from the OMOP provider tables to the National Provider Identifier (NPI) registry using name matching via a large language model (LLM).
comments.explanation: No details are provided about which LLM, what prompts, how outputs were validated, false positive/negative match rates, or edge case adjudication. The 85% fuzzy string matching threshold is mentioned without justification or sensitivity analysis. The entire radiologist-level analysis depends on correctly linking individuals across four data sources, making this a critical methodological step that is insufficiently described for reproducibility.

comments.title: Scan count discrepancy: 117,063 vs. 117,065 throughout the paper
comments.quote: Table 3 presents summary statistics for 117,065 CTPA scans across the pre-AI period (54,150 scans) and AI period (62,915 scans).
comments.explanation: The abstract, introduction, conclusion, and Table 1 state 117,063 CTPA scans, while Tables 3 and 5 report 117,065 (54,150 + 62,915 = 117,065). The difference of 2 likely reflects the two excluded single-scan sites, but the inconsistency across tables suggests Table 3 uses the pre-exclusion count while other sections use the post-exclusion count. The paper should be consistent about which number represents the analytic sample.

comments.title: Radiologist count inconsistencies: 388 vs. 389 (and other discrepancies across tables)
comments.quote: A total of 388 signing radiologists interpreted scans across all sites that eventually adopted the AI system.
comments.explanation: Table 1 reports 388 total unique signing radiologists, while Table 4 and the paper summary report 389. Additionally, Table 8 uses 169 radiologists in the AI period, Table 9 uses 153, Table 6 uses 148 (with AI engagement data), and Table 4 reports 187 in the AI period. The relationships between these different counts are never clarified. The drop from 169 to 153 (presumably due to missing engagement data) and the composition of each subsample deserve explanation for reproducibility.

comments.title: Per-radiologist volume doubling largely reflects fewer radiologists, not AI-driven productivity
comments.quote: even as total scan volume increases 16% from 54,150 to 62,915 scans
comments.explanation: Per-radiologist monthly volume nearly doubles (5.3 to 10.4, a ~96% increase), but total scan volume only increased 16%. The number of signing radiologists drops from 309 to 187 (a 39% decline). Much of the per-radiologist volume increase is mechanically driven by fewer radiologists handling a moderately larger total volume, yet the passage attributes productivity gains to AI-driven workflow optimization without mentioning the radiologist count reduction. This could mislead readers regarding AI's causal role in productivity.

comments.title: Hover duration is uniformly 1.00 minutes with zero variance, raising measurement questions
comments.quote: Hover duration is uniformly one minute across the sample.
comments.explanation: Table 6 shows hover duration with mean 1.00, SD 0.00, and IQR [1.00, 1.00]. Zero variation across 148 radiologists is implausible for a continuously measured time variable, strongly suggesting the system records at a fixed granularity, is capped/floored at 1 minute, or represents a binary indicator. This is never acknowledged, yet the engagement analysis that follows treats this as a meaningful metric. Readers cannot understand what this variable actually captures.

comments.title: Contradictory framing of AI as 'concurrent check' vs. triage tool
comments.quote: AI alerts arrive after radiologists have already opened cases in 99.7% of instances (median), indicating AI functions as a concurrent check rather than advance triage.
comments.explanation: The AI platform description states AI-positive scans are 'moved to top of queue' — explicitly a triage function. Yet the passage concludes AI is not functioning as triage because alerts arrive after case opening. Additionally, Table 6 reports a median 'AI triage lead' of 8.8 minutes, which seems inconsistent with the claim that radiologists open cases first 99.7% of the time. If the AI reprioritizes the queue, it performs triage even if the alert notification arrives after opening. The apparent contradiction between queue prioritization and the timing metric should be discussed.

comments.title: U-shaped disagreement pattern could be a compositional artifact of staggered rollout
comments.quote: The U-shaped pattern in the upper quantiles may indicate an initial period of excessive deference to AI followed by a shift toward more independent clinical judgment.
comments.explanation: With staggered rollout across 8 sites, the composition of radiologists contributing to each 'year after rollout' changes as new sites come online. The passage does not clarify whether 'year after rollout' is site-specific or system-wide, nor whether the radiologist pool is restricted to those present in all years. The U-shaped pattern (95th percentile: 65% → 30% → 50%) could reflect compositional changes from later-adopting sites entering the sample, differential attrition, COVID-19 effects, or interface changes — not necessarily genuine behavioral adaptation. These confounders should be flagged alongside the behavioral interpretation.

comments.title: Multiple copy-paste errors in conditional probability definitions across table notes
comments.quote: P(Rad- | AI-) is the probability the radiologist diagnoses no PE given the AI predicted PE
comments.explanation: Table notes for Tables 5, 7, 8, and 9 contain scrambled definitions of the conditional probabilities. For example, P(Rad-|AI-) is defined as conditioning on 'AI predicted PE' instead of 'AI predicted no PE'; P(Rad+|AI-) is described as 'diagnoses no PE' instead of 'diagnoses PE'; and P(Rad-|AI+) is given the definition of P(Rad-|AI-) in Table 9. These errors appear across multiple tables, suggesting systematic copy-paste issues. While the numerical values are internally consistent with correct definitions, readers consulting the notes would get wrong conditionals for multiple key variables.

comments.title: Engagement rate defined only over AI-positive notifications but used to stratify AI-negative disagreement
comments.quote: Engagement rate is calculated as the proportion of positive PE notifications that the radiologist hovered over before signing the report, averaged across all scans for each radiologist.
comments.explanation: The engagement rate captures hovering behavior only on AI-positive alerts, yet the analysis examines disagreement for both AI-positive and AI-negative predictions stratified by this metric. Since AI-negative cases produce only a grey indicator with no prioritization, engagement with negative predictions is unmeasured. The resulting non-monotonic U-shaped pattern (Q1 and Q4 have ~19% disagreement, Q2-Q3 have ~9-12%) is noteworthy but neither acknowledged nor discussed. The metric is a proxy for general AI receptiveness rather than a direct measure of interaction with all predictions, which should be stated.

comments.title: Decline in scans per patient may partly reflect right-censoring rather than a real change
comments.quote: Number of scans                   | 1.38                                       | 1.51                                         | 1.26
comments.explanation: The pre-AI period spans ~7 years (May 2012–Aug 2019) while the AI period spans ~3 years (Aug 2019–Jul 2022). Patients entering the AI period have less follow-up time to accumulate repeat scans. This right-censoring mechanically reduces average scans per patient. The authors present the decline (1.51 to 1.26) and the 10 pp reduction in multiple scans as suggesting 'AI may have reduced diagnostic uncertainty' without acknowledging this censoring issue.

comments.title: Inconsistent sample sizes in Figure 10 caption vs. Section 7 text
comments.quote: Figure 10A presents unconditional transition probabilities for all patients with a first scan (N = 84,640)... Figure 10B shows conditional transition probabilities among only those patients who returned for a second scan within 30 days (N = 4,095)
comments.explanation: Section 7 states the follow-up analysis uses 35,896 AI-period patients (1,375 with follow-up), but Figure 10's caption reports N = 84,640 (full sample including pre-AI) and N = 4,095 for follow-up patients. These are roughly triple the text-stated numbers. Either the figure uses the full sample while the text restricts to the AI period (without acknowledgment), or one contains an error. This is a significant discrepancy that affects interpretation of the transition analysis.

comments.title: Inconsistency in P(Rad-|AI+) between Table 7 overall and paper summary
comments.explanation: The paper summary states P(Rad-|AI+) = 15.9% and P(Rad-|AI-) = 97.2%, while Table 7's overall row reports 16.3% and 98.2% respectively. Table 8 reports yet different values (15.9% matching the summary). Since both appear to cover AI-period scans with AI predictions, the discrepancy is unexplained and could reflect different samples or weighting approaches.

comments.title: Equation (3) omits patient-level controls despite extensive covariate collection
comments.quote: $Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \text{Agree } (+)_i + \beta_2 \text{AI Only } (+)_i + \beta_3 \text{Rad Only } (+)_i + \gamma_s + \delta_{hd} + \lambda_t + \varepsilon_i$
comments.explanation: The authors collected 60 patient control variables (demographics, vitals, labs, comorbidities) specifically 'to test quasi-random assignment of patients to radiologists,' yet the outcome regression includes only care site, hour-of-day × day-of-week, and year fixed effects. Without patient-level controls, the agreement category coefficients conflate diagnostic concordance effects with patient severity selection. The paper does not explain why these covariates are excluded from the main specification.

comments.title: AI Only (+) mortality exceeding Agree (+) mortality deserves more careful discussion
comments.quote: Mortality increases 3.4 percentage points when both agree on PE, 4.0 percentage points when only AI detects PE, and 5.5 percentage points when only the radiologist detects PE.
comments.explanation: AI Only (+) 30-day mortality (4.0 pp increase) exceeds Agree (+) mortality (3.4 pp increase). If these patients truly have PE but are not treated because the radiologist rejected the AI flag, this implies radiologists are missing clinically consequential PEs that go untreated — a much stronger and more alarming finding than the measured tone suggests. The paper lists this as only one of three possible interpretations without flagging the clinical severity of this implication.

comments.title: Standard error of exactly 0.000 for Catheter-Associated Infection / Rad Only (+) coefficient
comments.explanation: A heteroskedasticity-robust standard error of exactly zero is essentially impossible for a regression coefficient. This likely reflects aggressive rounding of a very small number, but reporting se = 0.000 implies infinite precision. The associated Diff Test t-statistic of 1.57 seems inconsistent with one coefficient having near-zero standard error.

comments.title: 'All coefficients are precisely estimated' claim contradicts earlier acknowledgments of imprecision
comments.quote: Fourth, all coefficients are precisely estimated, including those in the AI Only (+) column.
comments.explanation: Earlier text explicitly notes that several pairwise differences are 'not precisely estimated' (e.g., 30-day mortality difference between AI Only and Rad Only). The blanket claim appears to refer to individual coefficients relative to the Agree(-) baseline rather than pairwise comparisons, but this distinction is not made explicit. Additionally, with rare outcomes (0.3-1.0% baseline rates) and small discordant-category samples (~570-610 observations each), precise estimation of all coefficients seems implausible.

comments.title: Restriction to suspected PE framed primarily as 'data feasibility' when substantive reasons also apply
comments.quote: This restriction reflects data feasibility. Identifying all incidental PE would require examining every chest CT scan regardless of indication.
comments.explanation: The authors justify excluding incidental PE solely on data feasibility grounds, but then note substantive clinical reasons (different presentations, treatment pathways). Since the OMOP database contains all chest CTs and the authors already use procedure codes to filter, the real reason is a combination of sample heterogeneity concerns and AI platform scope. Framing it purely as data feasibility understates the conceptual reasons and could mislead readers.

comments.title: Male + Female radiologist counts in Table 8 do not sum to Overall, with no explanation
comments.quote: Male | 101 | ... Female | 45 | ... Overall | 169 |
comments.explanation: 101 male + 45 female = 146 radiologists, but Overall shows 169, implying 23 (14%) have missing gender. Similarly, scan counts (35,621 + 14,268 = 49,889 vs. 53,880 Overall) leave 3,991 scans unaccounted for. The same issue appears for experience quartiles. This is not mentioned in table notes, and a reader would expect demographic panels to partition the overall sample.

comments.title: Outcome variable time windows and definitions embed causal assumptions without justification
comments.quote: Sudden cessation of cardiac activity requiring CPR or defibrillation, often due to severe PE blocking blood flow
comments.explanation: Outcome definitions in Table 11 embed causal language ('often due to severe PE,' 'caused by PE obstructing blood return') in what should be neutral variable definitions based on OMOP codes. The variables likely capture all events of each type, not PE-attributable events specifically. Additionally, cardiac arrest uses a 0-1 day window while similar acute complications use 0-30 days, and the recurrent PE window starts at day 8 — these specific boundaries lack clinical justification in the text and could materially affect results.

comments.title: Radiologist characteristics table (pre-AI: 309, AI: 187) implies large overlap but never clarifies composition
comments.quote: Table 4 presents summary statistics for 389 signing radiologists over the sample period, comparing the pre-AI (309 radiologists) and AI periods (187 radiologists).
comments.explanation: 309 + 187 = 496, meaning 107 radiologists appear in both periods. The text never clarifies this overlap, nor explains why only 187 of 389 radiologists appear in the AI period (roughly half apparently have no AI-period scans). This is a potentially important compositional issue that could affect interpretation of pre/post comparisons.

comments.title: Apparent mortality baseline coincidence between unconditional rate and Agree(-) conditional rate warrants verification
comments.quote: 30-day mortality rate             | 4.73
comments.explanation: Table 2 reports unconditional 30-day mortality of 4.73% across all 84,640 patients, while the outcomes discussion states the Agree(-) baseline is 4.7%. Since ~10% of scans are PE-positive with substantially higher mortality (3.4-5.5 pp above baseline), the unconditional mean should be noticeably higher than the Agree(-) baseline (~5.0-5.2%). The near-identical values (4.73% vs. 4.7%) are surprising and may warrant verification, though differences in sample composition could partially explain this.

comments.title: Exit rate comparison conflates PE diagnosis effect with disagreement effect
comments.quote: This 5.9 percentage point difference suggests that when radiologists override AI to diagnose PE, more follow-up scans are ordered (*t* = 2.78).
comments.explanation: The comparison between Rad Only (+) exit rate (90.6%) and Agree (-) exit rate (96.5%) conflates two factors: the presence of a PE diagnosis (which naturally leads to more follow-up) and the disagreement with AI. A more informative comparison would be Rad Only (+) vs. Agree (+) to isolate the override effect. Additionally, selecting the maximum and minimum exit rates across four categories for comparison without multiple comparison adjustment inflates significance.

comments.title: Inconsistency in ER vs. non-ER reporting: switches from disagreement to agreement framing mid-paragraph
comments.quote: When AI predicts no PE, radiologists agree in 98.39% of ER cases versus 97.80% of non-ER cases (*t* = 3.97, *p* < 0.001).
comments.explanation: The surrounding analysis consistently reports disagreement rates (e.g., 'radiologists disagree in 15.93% of ER cases'), but for AI-negative predictions the text switches to agreement rates without acknowledgment. This inconsistency in framing within the same paragraph could confuse readers.

comments.title: AI Only (+) cases take longest to diagnose despite AI triage, contradicting efficiency narrative
comments.quote: Hours from Order to Diagnosis               | 3.65
comments.explanation: The AI system moves AI-positive scans to the top of the queue, yet AI Only (+) cases have the longest order-to-diagnosis time (3.99 hours vs. 3.61 for Agree (+) and 3.78 for Rad Only (+)). Overall order-to-diagnosis time increases by 0.15 hours in the AI period, and PE-positive cases increase by 0.39 hours. This is not addressed in the context of the AI's triage function and contradicts typical AI triage efficiency claims.
